data.title: Magna International Non-GAAP EPS of $2.18 beats by $0.38, revenue of $10.85B beats by $310M
data.description:
• Magna International press release (MGA): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.18 beats by $0.38.
  • Revenue of $10.85B (+2.4% Y/Y) beats by $310M.

  • Magna expects solid top-line performance and sustained progress toward long-term margin objectives.

    • Sales expected to be between $41.9 billion and $43.5 billion vs $42.19B
    • Adjusted EBIT Margin expected between 6.0% and 6.6%
    • Adjusted diluted EPS expected to be in the range of $6.25 to $7.25
    • Capital spending projected to be between $1.5 billion and $1.6 billion
    • Free Cash Flow anticipated between $1.6 billion and $1.8 billion
    • Intends to repurchase remaining ∼22 million shares available under current buyback authorization (NCIB)
  • Shares +8% PM.

data.title: Roku stock jumps after Q4 beats, record premium subscription adds
data.description:
Roku (ROKU) shares jumped over 13% premarket on Friday after the streaming platform beat Wall Street expectations with its fourth-quarter results and issued an upbeat 2026 growth outlook.

The company reported a net profit of $80.5M, or $0.53 per share, in Q4, reversing a net loss of $35.5M a year earlier. Revenue rose 15.8% year over year to $1.39B, driven by an 18% increase in Platform segment revenue to $1.224B and a 3% gain in Devices revenue to $171M.

For full-year 2025, Roku generated a net profit of $88.36M (vs. a net loss of $129.38M in 2024) on total revenue of $4.737B (+15% Y/Y). The Platform segment—which includes advertising and streaming distribution—posted strong results in 2025, with revenue rising 18% to $4.145B. Growth was fueled by record fourth-quarter premium subscription net adds, driven in part by holiday promotions.

Streaming activity continued to climb in 2025, with users logging a total of 145.6B streaming hours for the year—up 15% from 2024. The company said it remains on pace to reach a key milestone of 100M global streaming households in 2026.

Looking ahead, Roku expects total revenue of $5.500B (vs. $5.48B estimated), gross profit of $2.435B, and adjusted EBITDA of $635M for full-year 2026. Platform revenue is projected to grow 18% Y/Y to $4.89B, while Devices revenue is seen growing low-single digits Y/Y to $610M.

“We are maintaining our focus on operational discipline, while continuing to invest in Platform growth. For OpEx, we expect it to be more heavily weighted in the second half of the year due to shifting retail distribution spend, but we still anticipate mid-single-digit YoY growth for the full year. We are executing against our strategy and enter 2026 well-positioned to drive sustained double-digit Platform growth and improving profitability,” the company said.

data.title: Euro Area trade surplus rises to €12.6B, GDP rises 0.3% in Q4
data.description:
• Euro Area GDP increased by 0.3% in Q4, versus the previous quarter, according to a flash estimate published by Eurostat, the statistical office of the European Union.
  • Y/Y, the GDP increased 1.3%, meeting estimates.
  • The first estimates of euro area balance showed a €12.6B surplus in trade in goods with the rest of the world in December 2025, compared with + €13.9B in December 2024.
  • The euro area exports of goods to the rest of the world in December 2025 were €234B, an increase of 3.4% compared with December 2024 (€226.3B).
  • Imports from the rest of the world stood at €221.3B, a rise of 4.2% compared with December 202

data.title: Corsair surges 25% after Q4 beat, plans up to $50M stock buyback
data.description:
Shares of Corsair (CRSR) surged about 25% on Friday after fourth quarter results and EPS outlook beat estimates.

Q4 Metrics

The company — which designs and sells gaming and streaming devices and components — saw its fourth quarter revenue climb about 6% year-over-year to $436.9M. For the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2025, adjusted EPS soared 87% year-over-year to $0.43. Both top and bottom line numbers surpassed analysts' estimates.

"Successful new product launches, smart inventory management, coupled with our supply chain flexibility, enabled us to navigate a memory-constrained market, shifting trade regulations and tariffs to deliver 62% growth in Q4 2025 adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA (855% growth in Q4 2025 GAAP net income), significantly outperforming our 6% year-over-year revenue growth." said Corsair's CEO Thi La.

In the fourth quarter, Gaming Components and Systems segment revenue grew over 11% year-over-year, led by 24% year-over-year growth in the company's memory business, with stable components and systems revenue.

Fourth quarter Gamer and Creator Peripherals Segment revenue fell 3% year-over-year, driven mainly by lower demand in North America, which was offset by stronger performance internationally, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region, according to Corsair.

Corsair's CFO Gordon Mattingly noted that in 2025 the company reduced its debt by $52.1M.

Stock Buyback

The company said its board authorized it to buy back up to $50M of its common shares. This is Corsair’s first repurchase authorization, according to the company. The program is effective immediately and does not have an expiration date.

"With this authorization, we now have additional flexibility to deploy our capital optimally – whether investing in organic growth, executing M&A, deleveraging the business, or returning capital to shareholders," said Mattingly.

Outlook

Corsair said that for the first quarter and full year 2026, it expects revenue to be down about 5% year-over-year at the assumed midpoint of the guided ranges. The company expects double-digit growth in its Gamer and Creator Peripherals segment, offset by a more cautious outlook for Gaming Components and Systems, driven by the ongoing global semiconductor shortages.

For the first quarter of 2026, the company expects net revenue to be in the range of $335M to $365M (midpoint at $350M).  Corsair expects non-GAAP EPS to be between $0.18 and $0.22 (midpoint at $0.20) versus a consensus EPS estimate of $0.15.

For the full year 2026, Corsair forecasted revenue to be between $1.33B and $1.47B (midpoint at $1.4B). The company expects non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of $0.58 to $0.74 (midpoint at $0.66) compared to a consensus EPS estimate of $0.65.

data.title: Stock index futures rise ahead of CPI data
data.description:
Stock index futures were mixed on Friday as traders awaited key retail inflation data due out ahead of the opening bell.

Dow futures (INDU) slipped 0.16% to 49,374. S&P 500 futures (SPX) were nearly flat, and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) edged higher by 0.18%.

Investors will digest the consumer price index report, with consensus expecting a 0.3% month-over-month increase, matching December’s pace. That would translate to a 2.5% year-over-year rise. Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, is forecast to climb 0.3% M/M and 2.5% Y/Y, down from the 2.6% pace in the prior month.

U.S. Treasury yields moved higher across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose to 4.13%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) ticked up to 4.75%. The shorter-end 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) climbed to 3.49%.

Top S&P 500 gainers in premarket trading included Arista Networks (ANET) +12.23%, Applied Materials (AMAT) +11.86%, and Coinbase Global (COIN) +6.58%. Decliners included Expedia Group (EXPE) -4.95%, Steel Dynamics (STLD) -3.26%, and Wynn Resorts (WYNN) -3.11%.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMAT, PINS, ROKU, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched lower in the premarket hours of Friday after yesterday's market slide, positioning ahead of critical CPI data.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Corsair Gaming (CRSR) +22% - Shares jumped after posting a solid Q4 beat, with non-GAAP EPS of $0.43 topping estimates by $0.16 and revenue rising 5.6% Y/Y to $436.9M, also ahead of expectations. While FY2026 revenue guidance came in below consensus ($1.33B–$1.47B vs. $1.58B), EPS guidance was broadly in line to higher, supported by expected double-digit growth in Gamer and Creator Peripherals, partially offset by a cautious outlook for Gaming Components amid global semiconductor constraints. Sentiment was further boosted by Corsair’s first-ever $50M share repurchase authorization.
  • Rivian Automotive (RIVN) +18% - Shares surged after reporting Q4 results and outlining its 2026 outlook, with investors looking past weaker automotive revenue, down 45% Y/Y due to lower regulatory credit sales, softer deliveries after tax credit expirations, and a lower ASP mix, and focusing instead on strong momentum in software and services, where revenue jumped 109% on contributions from its Volkswagen joint venture and higher service-related sales. While Q4 deliveries of 9,745 slightly missed consensus, Rivian said results were in line with internal expectations and guided to a meaningful pickup in 2026 deliveries of 62K–67K vehicles, alongside adjusted EBITDA of -$2.1B to -$1.8B and capex of about $2B.
  • Applied Materials (AMAT) +13% - Shares advanced after delivering FQ1 results and guidance that beat expectations, driven by accelerating AI-related chip investments. Management highlighted strong demand for leading-edge logic, high-bandwidth memory, and advanced packaging, areas where Applied is a market leader, and guided for FQ2 revenue of $7.15B–$8.15B and adjusted EPS of $2.44–$2.84, both comfortably ahead of Wall Street estimates.
  • Roku (ROKU) +13% - Shares rose after crushing Q4 earnings estimates and issuing upbeat 2026 guidance. The streaming platform provider reported Q4 adjusted EPS of $0.53, nearly double the expectations, on revenue of $1.38B (+16% Y/Y), which came in line. Strength was driven by platform revenue growth of 18% Y/Y, while management guided Q1 2026 revenue of $1.2B and full-year 2026 revenue of $5.5B, both ahead of consensus.

Biggest stock losers

  • Pinterest (PINS) -19% - Shares plunged after reporting weaker-than-expected Q4 results and issuing a soft Q1 2026 outlook. The company guided revenue in the range of $951M–$971M, missing the $981M consensus, implying 11%–14% Y/Y growth that includes a ~3-point FX tailwind. The company also forecast Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $166M–$186M.
  • DraftKings (DKNG) -15% - Shares sank after reporting mixed Q4 results and issuing FY2026 guidance that fell short of expectations. Q4 revenue rose 43.2% Y/Y to $1.99B, supported by strong customer engagement, efficient new customer acquisition, and higher sportsbook net revenue margins. Monthly unique paying customers were flat Y/Y at 4.8M, while average revenue per MUP jumped 43% to $143, driven by improved margins across both sportsbook and iGaming. DraftKings guided FY2026 revenue to $6.5B–$6.9B (midpoint $6.7B), well below the $7.3B consensus, overshadowing the solid quarterly performance.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Roku tops Q4 estimates; sees FY26 revenue outlook above forecast
Himax anticipates sales rebound in Q2 2026 as automotive and AI drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Himax Technologies, Inc. (HIMX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Karen Tiao reported, "Fourth quarter revenues registered $203.1 million, representing a sequential increase of 2.0%, better than our flat quarter-over-quarter guidance." Gross margin was 30.4%, and "Q4 profit per diluted ADS was 3.6 cents, at the high end of the guidance range of 2.0 to 4.0 cents." Revenue from large display drivers increased 14.2% from the previous quarter due to rush orders for TV and NB IC legacy products. Automotive driver IC sales for the full year 2025 grew single digit year-over-year, outpacing the broader market, while non-driver sales reached $42.3 million, a 7.9% increase from the previous quarter.
  • Tiao noted, "Q4 operating expenses were $54.9 million, a decrease of 9.6% from the previous quarter." Operating profit was $6.8 million, or an operating margin of 3.4%.
  • Tiao also stated, "Our year-end inventories were $152.7 million, an increase from $137.4 million last quarter but lower than $158.7 million a year ago."
  • Jordan Wu said, "We expect the first quarter to be the trough of the year, with sales rebounding in the second quarter and business momentum continuing to improve into the second half, supported by lean customer inventory levels and new projects for automotive customers scheduled to enter mass production later in the year."
  • Wu highlighted, "In DDIC and TDDI, we have already secured hundreds of design wins, commanding 40% market share in automotive DDIC and well over half in the global TDDI market." He added, "We believe the automotive market still offers significant upside potential, driven by rapid innovation and ongoing advancements in smart cabin as well as more vivid, intuitive and immersive displays."

Outlook

  • Tiao provided guidance for Q1 2026: "We expect Q1 revenues to decline 2.0% to 6.0% sequentially. Gross margin is expected to be flat to slightly down, depending on product mix. Q1 profit attributable to shareholders is estimated to be in the range of 2.0 to 4.0 cents per fully diluted ADS."
  • Wu indicated, "Our visibility for the whole year outlook of automotive sector remains limited amid the backdrop of uncertain government policy and consumer sentiment." However, he stated, "Continued growth in our non-driver IC businesses, particularly Tcon and WiseEye AI, should provide incremental support."
  • Wu noted the company expects CPO to become an important contributor to both revenue and profitability over the next few years, with mass-production readiness as a main goal for 2026 and meaningful contributions possible from 2027.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenues were $203.1 million, gross margin was 30.4%, and Q4 profit per diluted ADS was 3.6 cents. Operating profit was $6.8 million. Fourth quarter operating expenses were $54.9 million. Q4 after-tax profit was $6.3 million, or 3.6 cents per diluted ADS.
  • Full year 2025 revenues totaled $832.2 million, with a decline of 8.2% compared to 2024. Non-driver product sales increased 7.0% year-over-year.
  • The company reported $286.2 million of cash, cash equivalents and other financial assets as of December 31, 2025. Q4 operating cash inflow was $16.8 million.

Q&A

  • Tiffany Yeh, Morgan Stanley: "May I know why the margin would be flat to down quarter-over-quarter in first quarter. Is it because of product mix or are we seeing elevated pressure coming from like the increasing material costs and also the OSAT costs?" Jordan Wu: "We are only guiding for a flat to slight decline only, so we are not seeing material change from the gross margin of last quarter and the difference is really the product mix change... We are in discussion with both our customer side and vendor side. But that is not really quite a factor for our Q1 gross margin guidance. If anything, I think that is going to becoming a factor starting from Q2 and onward."
  • Yeh: "Could you give us more details or maybe some guidance for the CPO revenue in maybe 2026 and 2027, as I think investors are very excited about our development and progress in this area." Wu: "The main goal of 2026 for us and also for our partner FOCI is to complete the validation of both our Gen 1 and Gen 2 products... meaning we are talking about sample shipments only. Notably...we can potentially see meaningful top and bottom-line contribution starting from 2027, even before the official MP gets started."
  • Online inquiry: "Our OLED sales, is it going to be huge in 2026, its price or price premium versus conventional panels?" Wu: "Our expected sales contribution for 2026 is still less than 10% of our total sales. So I would say probably high single digits of contribution, 2026. The ramping, the real ramping is going to be in 2027... For Himax, the OLED product’s gross margin for smartphone, it is actually lower than our company average... However, I would say something very different for automotive OLED and IT OLED. These two areas are our focus area right now, and they both enjoy much better gross margin compared to our traditional LCD products."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral tone, focusing on margin guidance and the ramp of new businesses, with questions directly probing cost pressure and revenue timing for new products.
  • Management responses reflected confidence, especially in automotive and AI-related segments, while acknowledging ongoing uncertainties. Wu stated, "We are only guiding for a flat to slight decline only," and "We believe the automotive market still offers significant upside potential."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was consistent—confident in long-term prospects, but measured in addressing near-term uncertainties. Analysts in both quarters sought clarity on margins and CPO ramp, maintaining a pragmatic approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 revenue increased 2.0% sequentially, outperforming flat guidance, while gross margin was stable. Operating profit turned positive from a loss in Q3, reflecting improved profitability and cost controls.
  • Guidance for Q1 2026 is for a sequential revenue decline of 2.0% to 6.0%, similar to cautious outlook from Q3, but now management anticipates a rebound in Q2, highlighting a shift toward optimism for the second half.
  • Automotive and AI (WiseEye) remain key focus areas, but more emphasis was placed this quarter on the timing and contribution of CPO and OLED ramp, particularly into 2027.
  • Analysts’ focus remained on margin and new business ramp, with ongoing questions about cost pressures and revenue timing.
  • Management confidence in automotive and non-driver IC businesses, as well as future CPO contributions, remained steady, while near-term caution persisted.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, memory price volatility, and limited visibility in the automotive sector as key risks.
  • Wu said, "We are in discussion with both our customer side and vendor side" regarding potential price increases due to rising foundry and material costs, but noted these are not expected to materially impact Q1 margin.
  • The timing of CPO ramp-up and the scale of OLED adoption, especially in automotive and IT, depend on broader industry developments and customer decisions.

Final Takeaway

Himax's Q4 2025 results delivered sequential revenue growth and a return to operating profit at the high end of guidance, driven by strength in large display and automotive ICs despite macroeconomic uncertainty. Management expects Q1 2026 to be the trough, with a sales rebound anticipated in Q2 and momentum in the second half, supported by automotive project ramps and expanding AI and non-driver IC contributions. The company continues to prioritize advanced automotive, AI, and CPO solutions, positioning for significant growth in 2027 as new technologies enter mass production and customer adoption accelerates.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ryan Specialty signals $80M annual savings from Empower restructuring as property pressures persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ryan Specialty Holdings (RYAN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Patrick Ryan, Founder & Executive Chairman, stated that “for the quarter, we delivered organic growth of 6.6%” and highlighted surpassing “revenues of $3 billion, up 21% year-over-year, driven by organic growth of 10.1%, on top of 12.8% in 2024, and significant contributions from our M&A strategy.” He noted, “we completed 5 acquisitions with trailing revenue of over $125 million” and emphasized a deliberate decision to diversify with “delegated authority, including both binding authority, and underwriting management.” Ryan announced the launch of Empower, a 3-year restructuring program, anticipating “a cumulative special charge of approximately $160 million through 2028” and expecting “the program will deliver approximately $80 million of annual savings in 2029.”

  • Ryan also shared that the board authorized a $300 million share repurchase program, saying, “this decision reflects our view that there’s a meaningful dislocation between our current valuation and our confidence in the near and long-term outlook of our business.”

  • CEO Timothy Turner described 2025 as “the most challenging property environment the insurance industry has faced in decades,” reporting that “the fourth quarter was particularly challenging. We saw a further decline in property pricing as the quarter progressed…it was most notable in the month of December, particularly on certain large accounts where pricing was down 25% to 35%.” He detailed continued optimism in high hazard lines and noted that “our delegated authority business manages north of $10 billion in premium across more than 300 products.”

  • CFO Janice Hamilton reported, “In Q4, total revenue grew 13% period-over-period to $751 million,” with “organic revenue growth of 6.6%” and M&A adding “over 5 percentage points to our top line.” She shared, “Adjusted EBITDAC grew 2.9% to $222 million. Adjusted EBITDAC margin was 29.6% compared to 32.6% in the prior year period. Adjusted diluted earnings per share of $0.45 was comparable period-over-period.” Hamilton confirmed an 8% increase to the regular quarterly dividend for Class A stockholders and reiterated the $300 million share repurchase program.

Outlook

  • The company guides to organic revenue growth “in the high single digits for 2026,” with Hamilton stating, “this reflects our current view of market conditions, including continued property pricing pressures, a more moderate pace of casualty growth and broader macroeconomic uncertainty.” She noted, “for the full year 2026, we are guiding to an adjusted EBITDAC margin of flat to moderately down as compared to the prior year.” Hamilton also projected, “we expect the program will deliver approximately $80 million of annual savings in 2029.”

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, total revenue was $751 million, with organic revenue growth of 6.6%. Adjusted EBITDAC reached $222 million, and the adjusted EBITDAC margin was 29.6%. Adjusted diluted earnings per share stood at $0.45. For the full year, total revenue surpassed $3 billion, adjusted EBITDAC was $967 million, and adjusted EPS was $1.96. The company ended the quarter at 3.2x total net leverage.

  • M&A contributed to revenue growth, with five acquisitions adding over $125 million in trailing revenue. The board approved both an 8% dividend increase and a $300 million share repurchase program.

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: Asked about the reduction from double-digit organic growth guidance to high single digits, especially given property price declines. Hamilton explained, “the fourth quarter really marked an intensification of some of these property pricing trends. We saw particularly in the large accounts, rate decreases to 25% to 35%, which was higher than what we were seeing earlier in the year…that strong growth that we experienced in 2025, we expect to moderate within 2026.” Turner added, “we believe there's plenty of new business for us to capture this year, and we continue to look for new innovative ways to broker that business and underwrite it.”

  • Greenspan also raised concerns about AI’s impact on brokers. Ryan responded, “we look at AI as an ally, not as an adversary. Lots of opportunities for us to embrace AI and improve…we also believe that there's going to be some significant efficiencies through AI. We can't quantify them at this time.”

  • Taylor Scott, Barclays: Inquired about construction market headwinds and the share repurchase. Turner clarified, “the construction segment and practice group for us remains very, very strong…macro-economic pressure and the interest rates have slowed down the timeline between submit to quote to bind.” Hamilton added, “just given the continued uncertainty from a macroeconomic perspective, it is still early…to tell…how that will play out in '26.”

  • Brian Meredith, UBS: Asked about client demand and margin outlook. Turner stated, “we don't see any measurable trends of buying less…structure demands are a little bit different. So higher retention levels in certain accounts, alternative risk…comes into play.” Hamilton noted, “high single digits. We are pleased with that expectation for '26. We believe that, that will be industry-leading growth.”

  • Meyer Shields, KBW: Questioned the deferred 35% margin target. Hamilton answered, “we deferred the goal of the 35% margin target beyond 2027…Project Empower is intended to support the efficiencies…to contribute to that modest margin expansion in most years. But at this point, we're not putting a time line around it.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on growth guidance, margin outlook, and AI-related risks, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone as they probed for risks and sustainability of performance.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, but during Q&A, explanations became more measured, particularly when discussing headwinds in property and margin expansion timelines. Phrases such as “we believe…” and “we expect…” indicated a cautious approach.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more cautious regarding growth rates and margin expansion, especially as the company shifted from double-digit to high single-digit growth guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from double-digit organic growth in the previous quarter to high single digits for 2026, reflecting intensified property price declines and moderating casualty growth.

  • Margin guidance remains “flat to moderately down,” continuing the trend of near-term margin pressure due to talent and technology investments; the 35% margin target remains deferred.

  • The previous quarter focused on aggressive talent acquisition and M&A; this quarter announced the Empower restructuring program to drive efficiency and future savings.

  • Analyst questions in both quarters focused on growth sustainability, margin outlook, and impacts of industry trends such as AI and market competition, but concerns about property headwinds and pace of margin improvement were more prominent this quarter.

  • Management’s optimism about growth opportunities and diversification was more tempered this quarter in response to ongoing market volatility and competitive pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Intensified property pricing declines, especially in large accounts, with rate decreases of 25% to 35% in December.

  • Ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty impacting large construction projects and overall business mix.

  • Margin pressures from recent talent and technology investments, with Empower aimed at mitigating long-term cost growth.

  • Analyst concerns about the potential for AI-driven disruption and the sustainability of profit commissions in a softening market.

Final Takeaway

Ryan Specialty Holdings delivered strong full-year results despite significant headwinds, with leadership emphasizing diversification, continued investment in talent and technology, and the launch of the Empower program to drive future efficiency and margin improvements. The company guides to high single-digit organic growth for 2026 and anticipates $80 million in annual savings from Empower starting in 2029, while maintaining flexibility in capital allocation through M&A and the newly authorized share repurchase program. Management remains committed to industry-leading growth, though near-term momentum is expected to moderate amid ongoing property market pressures and macroeconomic uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ryan Specialty Group raises dividend by 8.3% to $0.13
data.description:
• Ryan Specialty Group (RYAN) declares $0.13/share quarterly dividend, 8.3% increase from prior dividend of $0.12.
  • Forward yield 1.17%
  • Payable March 10; for shareholders of record Feb. 24; ex-div Feb. 24.
  •  A portion of the dividend, $0.06 per share, will be funded by free cash flow from Ryan Specialty, LLC and will be paid to all holders of the Company’s Class A common stock and the holders of the LLC Common Units
  • See RYAN Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.
